title: Listening - Part 4: Designing Sets for the Theatre
description: You will hear part of a radio interview with a woman called Lucy, who designs theatre sets. For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

content.audio_script: Presenter: We’re talking today to Lucy Grant, who designs stage sets for theatre productions. Lucy, did you study theatre design from the beginning? || Lucy: No, I started a degree in architecture because I enjoyed drawing buildings. During my second year, a student drama group needed someone to create a room on stage using almost no money. [24]I enjoyed producing an imaginary place far more than designing a building that might never be constructed[/24]. After that production, I changed direction and trained for theatre. || Presenter: Is designing a set mainly about making it look impressive? || Lucy: A striking image matters, but actors need to use the space safely and naturally. A beautiful staircase is useless if costumes make it impossible to climb quickly. [25]My first question is always what movements the performers must make during the play[/25]. Appearance develops alongside those practical requirements. || Presenter: How do you begin a new production? || Lucy: I read the script several times and talk to the director about the mood. I used to start drawing straight away, but that sometimes meant wasting effort. [26]Now I build a small three-dimensional model so the director and lighting designer can discuss ideas before full-size scenery is made[/26]. It prevents misunderstandings later. || Presenter: Do you always aim for historical accuracy? || Lucy: It depends on the production. We recently staged a story set in the nineteenth century, but the director wanted the audience to connect it with modern life. [27]We included a few modern materials deliberately, because the aim was to suggest the story still had relevance today[/27]. Some audience members found it surprising, but most understood. || Presenter: What is difficult about working in a small theatre? || Lucy: Storage and money are limited, so we cannot throw everything away after a show. I actually enjoy that challenge. [28]I often reuse pieces of scenery from earlier productions, changing their surface or shape so that audiences do not recognise them[/28]. It is good for the budget and the environment. || Presenter: Have you ever had a disaster just before a performance? || Lucy: Once, a large painted wall would not fit through the backstage door on the afternoon of the first show. I was horrified. [29]The team divided it into sections and joined them again on stage, and the audience never knew there had been a problem[/29]. That taught me the value of calm teamwork. || Presenter: What should young people interested in set design do? || Lucy: Drawing helps, and computer design is increasingly useful. But you cannot understand theatre only on a screen. [30]Volunteer backstage wherever you can, because watching rehearsals teaches you how scenery actually needs to work[/30]. Practical experience is as important as artistic talent.

task.title: Part 4
task.instruction: For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

questions.question: Why did Lucy decide to work in theatre design?

options.A: Her architecture course included theatre projects.
options.B: She preferred creating a fictional space for a performance.
options.C: She wanted her designs to cost less to produce.

questions.explanation: Lucy found creating an imaginary stage location more enjoyable than designing possible buildings.

questions.question: What does Lucy consider first when designing a set?

options.A: how the actors will use the space
options.B: whether the audience will find it impressive
options.C: how quickly the costumes can be changed

questions.explanation: Lucy first considers the movements performers need to make during the play.

questions.question: Why does Lucy now produce a model of a set?

options.A: To allow ideas to be discussed before construction begins.
options.B: To show theatre managers how much the set will cost.
options.C: To help actors practise without attending rehearsals.

questions.explanation: The model lets the director and lighting designer discuss ideas before full-size construction.

questions.question: Why did Lucy use modern materials in a historical production?

options.B: The production was not intended for a theatre stage.
options.C: They helped connect the story with the present day.

questions.explanation: The modern elements were intended to suggest that the old story remains relevant today.

questions.question: What does Lucy say about working in a small theatre?

options.A: She finds its limited storage frustrating.
options.B: She modifies previously used scenery for new shows.
options.C: She avoids designing scenery that cannot be recycled.

questions.explanation: She often reuses and changes scenery so it looks different in a new production.

questions.question: What happened when a wall would not fit backstage?

options.A: The performance began later than planned.
options.B: The scenery was removed from the production.
options.C: The team solved the problem without the audience noticing.

questions.explanation: They cut and rejoined the wall on stage, and the audience did not know.

questions.question: What does Lucy recommend to future set designers?

options.A: Gain practical experience behind the scenes.
options.B: Learn computer design before learning to draw.

questions.explanation: Lucy recommends volunteering backstage because rehearsals show how scenery must function.
